prompt: What is the main objective of GRC reporting for executive stakeholders?
choices: Present risk, compliance, and audit posture trends that support governance decisions and resource prioritization
List every open task without severity or ownership context
Display only policy document counts by department
Replace risk reviews with dashboard color indicators
explanation: Executive GRC reporting should translate operational data into decision-ready governance insight.

prompt: Which report design best supports board-level RC discussions?
choices: Trend-based dashboards showing inherent versus residual risk, control effectiveness, and high-risk exception aging
A single export of all risk records with raw notes
Daily analyst activity counts without risk segmentation
Unfiltered policy attestation comments
explanation: Board reporting should emphasize trend, impact, and accountability rather than raw record volume.

prompt: How should GRC reporting handle inconsistent data quality across business units?
choices: Include data-quality indicators, ownership gaps, and remediation plans alongside risk and compliance metrics
Exclude low-quality units from all dashboards permanently
Average all scores without transparency to improve appearance
Delay reporting until every field is perfectly complete
explanation: Transparent data-quality dimensions preserve trust and drive remediation accountability.

prompt: Which cadence model is most effective for GRC reporting?
choices: Operational weekly dashboards with monthly governance packs and quarterly executive summaries
Annual static reports for all audiences
On-demand reporting only after major incidents
Separate inconsistent report definitions per team
explanation: Layered cadence supports tactical action and strategic governance with consistent definitions.

prompt: Which outcome indicates GRC reporting maturity?
choices: Leaders consistently use reports to reduce exposure, close exceptions faster, and improve control performance
More report variants with conflicting risk numbers
Longer time spent reconciling spreadsheets before reviews
Higher percentage of unowned high-risk findings
explanation: Mature reporting drives measurable governance outcomes, not just dashboard production.

prompt: Which role is primarily accountable for coordinating end-to-end response actions during an active security incident?
choices: Security incident coordinator or incident commander
CMDB data steward
Change advisory board chair
Procurement specialist
explanation: The incident coordinator drives response orchestration, communication, and decision pacing.

prompt: What is the core responsibility of a Tier 1 SOC analyst in the SIR role model?
choices: Perform initial triage, enrichment, and prioritization before escalation
Approve all risk acceptances for the organization
Own final incident closure sign-off for legal review
Manage CMDB class hierarchy changes
explanation: Tier 1 analysts focus on fast, accurate triage and routing to the right responders.

prompt: How should SIR implementations enforce role separation between responders and approvers?
choices: Use role-based access controls and workflow approvals to separate execution from governance decisions
Allow all responders full administrative permissions by default
Assign one shared account to all SOC roles
Restrict approvals to manual email only
explanation: Role separation improves accountability and reduces governance risk in high-impact incidents.

prompt: Which role typically validates technical eradication before case closure?
choices: Assigned resolver or containment owner with required reviewer sign-off
Service catalog manager
Payroll systems administrator
Knowledge base contributor
explanation: Technical owners verify eradication outcomes, while governance sign-off confirms closure readiness.

prompt: Which KPI most directly indicates healthy SIR role clarity?
choices: Lower reassignment churn and faster handoff times between triage, containment, and recovery roles
Higher number of incidents with no assignment group
More incidents reopened due to unclear ownership
Longer approval wait times for routine decisions
explanation: Clear roles reduce handoff friction and improve response continuity.

prompt: What defines a bottom-up Service Mapping approach?
choices: Start from infrastructure and application component dependencies, then assemble validated service context upward
Start only from executive service catalogs and skip technical dependency discovery
Map by importing static architecture diagrams without CMDB records
Limit mapping to network devices and ignore applications
explanation: Bottom-up mapping builds service understanding from discovered technical relationships.

prompt: When is bottom-up mapping especially useful?
choices: When business service definitions are incomplete but technical dependency data is available
When all services already have perfectly maintained maps
When discovery is intentionally disabled across environments
When no CMDB classes exist for application components
explanation: Bottom-up techniques help bootstrap service context from reliable technical evidence.

prompt: Which prerequisite most improves bottom-up mapping accuracy?
choices: Consistent CI identification, reconciliation rules, and discovered relationship quality in CMDB
Weekly manual deletion of stale CI relationships
Mapping only production databases regardless service boundaries
Removing ownership and support group fields from CIs
explanation: Bottom-up mapping quality depends on strong CMDB identity and relationship integrity.

prompt: How should teams convert bottom-up technical maps into business-service-aligned views?
choices: Layer application service definitions and ownership metadata over validated dependency clusters
Publish technical maps directly to executives without context
Remove non-critical dependencies to simplify every map
Create separate maps per admin without shared standards
explanation: Business relevance comes from overlaying ownership and service definitions onto technical topology.

prompt: Which KPI indicates bottom-up mapping is delivering operational value?
choices: Improved incident impact accuracy and faster root-cause isolation due to dependable dependency topology
More manual reassignment of application CI ownership
Higher count of unclassified CIs in mapped services
Longer mean time to restore with no map updates
explanation: Operational value appears when topology quality improves impact and troubleshooting outcomes.
